title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: California LCFS Credit Prices Slide Further as Type 1 Trading Witness Uptick WoW
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights

(2nd June â€“ 8th June)
  • A total of 297,000 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 54.57% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a fall of 41.67% WoW as 865,809 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $44.97/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 5.05% fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 4.81% in price, reaching $52.61/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price fell by 6.60% WoW; on 17th June, the spot price stood at $44.38/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $47.90/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 2.21% WoW. D4, D5 RIN prices increased by 8.99% WoW, while D6 RIN prices went up by 10.11%.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

Type 1 Trade Prices and spot prices declined last week, continuing their downward trend since CARB resubmitted the LCFS amendments to the OAL. Last week, President Trump revoked Californiaâ€™s authority to set vehicle emission standards, including its EV mandate, triggering a lawsuit from California and ten other states. The move could delay the stateâ€™s climate targets under the LCFS. Meanwhile, Washington proposed updates to its Clean Fuel Standard, focusing on sustainable aviation fuels and zero-emission heavy-duty truck infrastructure. The rulemaking proposal promotes the production and use of alternative jet fuel, also known as sustainable aviation fuel, in Washington. The proposal would also strengthen incentives for investing in charging equipment for zero-emission heavy-duty trucks. Public meetings on the rule are scheduled for late July, with comments open until August 1, 2025. Last week on Friday, the The EPA has proposed reducing the 2025 cellulosic biofuel volume requirement from 1.38 B RINs to 1.19 B RINs due to an anticipated shortfall in production. Under the proposed rule, renewable fuel produced using imported feedstockâ€”or imported directlyâ€”would generate only half the number of RINs compared to domestically produced fuel. Additionally, renewable electricity (eRINs) would no longer qualify as a renewable fuel. The proposal also includes a shift in biomass-based volume requirements from physical gallons to RINs to better align with the overall Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) system. A virtual public hearing is scheduled for July 8, 2025, with a public comment period open until August 8. The proposed volume requirements under each D-code are listed below: [caption id="attachment_598841" align="alignnone" width="702"] Source: United States Environmental Protection Agency[/caption]

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

The average Type 1 prices recorded a 5.05% WoW fall, decreasing from $47.36 to $44.97 this week, while all non-zero credit prices decreased by 4.81% WoW, from $55.27 to $52.61. Type 1 trades saw 297,000 credits exchanged, reflecting a rise of 54.57% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a fall of 41.67%, reaching 865,809 credits. Â  [caption id="attachment_598833" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

Â CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 6.60% WoW, reaching $41.70/MT. On 17th June, the spot price was $44.38/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $47.90/MT as of 17th June. Â  [caption id="attachment_598834" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption]

Â CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

As per the data reported by CARB last week, 73 trades were reported in the market, with 5 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $80.00 for one Type 2 transaction at a volume of 15,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $40.00 per credit of Type 1 reported for one transaction at a volume of 35,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 100,000 credits of the Type 2 category were undertaken for a price of $76.75, and the smallest volume transfers of 1,000 credits of the Type 1 category were undertaken for a price of $42.75. [caption id="attachment_598835" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

The average spot price in June for LCFS Type 1 credits stood at $45.91 through the week of June 9th, and the total number of trading volumes transacted for the same period stood at 2.35 MMT. [caption id="attachment_598836" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption]

Â CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 9th June â€“ 15th June, total VPT recorded a 13.05% decline WoW, with a fall in all type 1 and non-zero credit prices. [caption id="attachment_598837" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption]

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 2.21% WoW. D4, D5 RIN prices increased by 8.99% WoW, while D6 RIN prices went up by 10.11%. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Â Upcoming Dates

Â Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week
  • Grassley Discusses Trade Deals, 45Z and Tax Cut Extensions with Treasury Secretary Bessent: During a Senate Finance Committee hearing today, Sen. Chuck Grassley (R-Iowa), a senior member and former chairman of the committee, questioned Department of the Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent about the importance of finalizing trade deals to provide certainty and the need for Treasury to work with Congress and farmer-led organizations to get 45Z regulations right for farmers.
  • DEQ to Exercise Enforcement Discretion for 2026 Model Year Under Oregonâ€™s Advanced Clean Cars II Rule: Oregon Department of Environmental Quality Director Leah Feldon issued a memo directing the agency to exercise limited enforcement discretion for Oregonâ€™s Advanced Clean Cars II Rule, also known as ACC II. Effective immediately, DEQ will not pursue enforcement or assess penalties against vehicle manufacturers that do not meet zero-emissions vehicle sales targets for Model Year 2026 passenger cars and light-duty trucks.
  • Growth Energy: EPAâ€™s RVO Proposal Upholds Commitment to Biofuels, Strengthens Rural America: Growth Energy, the nationâ€™s largest biofuel trade association, issued the following statement after the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) announced its latest renewable volume obligations (RVOs) under the Renewable Fuel Standard. EPAâ€™s RVO proposal applies to 2026 and 2027, stipulating that the nationâ€™s refiners must blend 15 billion gallons of conventional biofuels into the nationâ€™s fuel blend for each plan year.
